segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black screen displaying the white Cyrillic text 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' as a whimsical, accordion-like melody begins to play. The scene transitions to a sunlit stairwell with a patterned floor and an arched window overlooking city buildings. A gray striped cat with a white muzzle and chest sits on the windowsill, looking out, then turns and jumps down to the floor, moving toward the window, all while the playful melody continues. The title card 'ТРОЕ ИЗ ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' appears in bold yellow letters on a blue background, accompanied by a more prominent and upbeat theme song. Credits follow, listing the scriptwriter, director, composer, and other crew members in yellow Cyrillic text on the same blue background, with the cheerful theme music continuing. After the credits, the scene returns to the stairwell, where a red-haired boy in a yellow shirt and blue pants descends the stairs, holding a piece of food. He stops and looks up at the cat, who is now perched on the windowsill again, smiling. The boy gestures toward the cat, seemingly offering the food, as the cat looks down at him with a friendly expression and a male voice states, "Неправильно ты, дядя Фёдор, бутерброд ешь. Ты его..." (You're eating your sandwich wrong, Uncle Fyodor. You...).

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with a red-haired boy, identified as 'Дядя Фёдор' (Uncle Fyodor) by his own voice, wearing a yellow shirt and blue pants, standing by a window and offering food to a gray striped cat, 'Кот Матроскин' (Cat Matroskin), who sits on the windowsill. The cat, speaking in a distinct male voice, advises the boy on how to properly hold the sausage for better taste, explaining he knows everyone in the house because he lives in the attic and can see everything, also revealing his attic is currently under renovation, leaving him nowhere to live. Initially hesitant and appearing stubborn, the cat's expression softens after the boy's smiling gesture, and he accepts the food with a pleased look. The boy then asks if he wants more food, confirming there's more at home, and invites the cat to live with him. The cat expresses concern that the boy's mother might object, but the boy reassures him, suggesting his father would stand up for them. The scene transitions to a cozy living room with red curtains, where 'Дядя Фёдор' is now on a green couch, reading a book with a cat on the cover, while 'Кот Матроскин' sits beside him, wrapped in a black and white striped blanket, eating from a plate with a milk bottle nearby. Gentle, whimsical instrumental music begins to play, underscoring the shift from cautious interaction to warm companionship and a growing bond between the boy and the cat.

segments.integrated_caption: A whimsical, slightly melancholic instrumental tune plays as the scene opens in a cozy living room, where a red-haired boy reads a book with a cat cover on a green couch. Beside him, a gray striped cat, wrapped in a black and white striped blanket, eats from a plate with a milk bottle, having initially appeared hesitant before accepting food from the boy's gesture. The door then opens, and a red-haired man in a green sweater and a red-haired woman with glasses and a pink blouse enter, smiling. The woman's voice is heard remarking, "Something smells like cat spirit here. No doubt, Uncle Fyodor brought the cat." The man's voice responds, "So what? One cat won't bother us," to which the woman retorts, "Well, it won't bother you, but it will bother me." The man moves to a white tablecloth-covered table, placing a loaf of bread and a milk bottle, while the woman walks to a wooden cabinet, holding a white cloth and gesturing as if giving instructions. The woman then questions the cat's usefulness, asking, "What's the use of this cat?" The man counters, "Why necessarily use? What's the use of this painting on the wall, for example?" as the camera focuses on a framed still-life painting above the couch. The woman explains, "But from this painting on the wall, there's a very big use. It covers a hole in the wallpaper." The man insists, "And the cat will be useful. It can catch mice," but the woman states, "We don't have mice." The man playfully suggests, "And we'll get some." The family gathers around the couch, with the man pointing at the painting and the woman smiling and gesturing toward the cat. The woman then gives an ultimatum, "If this cat is so important to you, please choose: either him or me," to which the man responds with a placating "No, no, no, oh, oh." The man then playfully jumps onto the couch, landing beside the boy and cat, as the woman watches with amusement, maintaining a warm and familial atmosphere despite the playful debate.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with cheerful, whimsical instrumental music as a close-up shows a red-covered book titled 'КУДА' (Where To) featuring a black cat on its cover. A red-haired boy, identified as Fyodor, holds the book while sitting on a green couch beside a gray striped cat, who is wrapped in a black and white striped blanket and eating from a plate with a milk bottle nearby. Fyodor's young voice is heard saying, "Well, I choose you. I've known you for a long time," possibly referring to the cat or the book. The scene transitions to a wider shot of the living room as a red-haired man and red-haired woman enter, smiling and gesturing toward Fyodor and the cat. The man playfully jumps onto the green couch, causing the cat to flinch, while the woman stands by a wooden cabinet, holding a white cloth. Fyodor, now standing, holds the book and looks up at his parents with a slightly embarrassed expression, then looks down, appearing thoughtful or sad. The scene shifts to a different room where Fyodor is kneeling on a stool at a white tablecloth-covered table, intently writing on a large sheet of paper with a red pen. His voice narrates a letter to his "dear parents, dad and mom," stating, "I love you very much, but I also love animals, especially all sorts of cats!" A deeper voice (likely the red-haired man) interjects, confirming the boy's sentiment about cats. Fyodor continues, "But you don't let me have them. Therefore, I'm leaving for the village and I will live there." The gray striped cat sits beside him, mischievously rummaging through a brown sack and pulling out various kitchen items like a blue kettle and a yellow pot, occasionally glancing at the focused boy. The cat then playfully knocks over the kettle, causing a small spill, but Fyodor continues writing without interruption, signing off, "Your son, Uncle Fyodor." The deeper voice adds, "And the cat." The scene concludes with a wide outdoor shot of a rural village by a river, with colorful houses and a large white building atop a green hill, suggesting the destination of Fyodor's journey.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a cheerful, folksy musical backdrop, a rural landscape unfolds with a dirt path leading to a village, marked by a sign reading 'ПРОСТОКВАШИНО 100 м'. The red-haired boy, D дя Фёдор, dressed in a yellow shirt and blue pants, stands on the path holding a wooden staff, accompanied by the gray striped cat, Matroskin, who carries a brown sack on a stick over his shoulder. A brown dog, Sharik, with floppy ears and a white muzzle, suddenly runs up the path, stopping to sit and look up at them with an expressive, pleading face. Sharik, in an eager male voice, greets them and asks, "Здравствуйте! Возьмите меня к себе жить! Я вам буду всё охранять." Matroskin, with a slightly sarcastic male voice, retorts, "Ещё чего. Мы же сами нигде не живём. Так нам через год прибегай, когда мы хозяйством мы бы заведёмся." D дя Фёдор gently pats Sharik’s head, telling Matroskin to be quiet, stating, "Хороший собака ещё никому не мешала," and suggests, "Давай лучше узнаем, нет ли в деревне свободного домика." Sharik eagerly confirms, "Да есть, есть! Я вам один дом покажу. Там никто не живёт. Хозяева за реку переехали." Matroskin inquires, "А печка там есть?" to which Sharik enthusiastically replies, "У! В полкухни." Sharik then asks again if they will take him, and D дя Фёдор agrees, "Возьмём." Matroskin adds, "Возьмём! Втроём веселее." D дя Фёдор asks Sharik's name, who introduces himself as "Шарик. Я из простых собак, не из породистых." D дя Фёдор then introduces himself, "А меня Дядя Фёдор зовут," and Matroskin adds, "А кот Матроскин," clarifying, "Фамилия такой." Sharik responds, "Очень приятно." The dog then stands up, wagging its tail, and the boy, cat, and dog continue their journey, walking away from the camera along the path toward the village, set against green hills, a river, and colorful houses, creating a pastoral atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a cheerful, whimsical accordion-like tune accompanying an exterior shot of a rustic wooden house, marked by a sign reading 'House is free, live whoever wants to'. Inside, the gray striped cat and brown dog playfully interact near a fireplace, holding a broom and stick respectively, as the red-haired boy enters with a duster and begins cleaning the simply furnished room. The mischievous cat hides behind a green curtain before reappearing with a grin. A man with a mustache and brown hat then peeks through the window, smiling and waving. The boy, dog, and cat look towards the man, who greets them with a distinct voice, saying "Будьте здоровы!" (Be healthy!), to which the boy responds "Спасибо!" (Thank you!). The man then questions the boy, asking "And you, boy, whose are you? Where did you come from to our village?", and the boy replies, "I'm nobody's. I'm on my own, boy. My own. I came from the city." The man expresses disbelief, stating, "That doesn't happen. For children to be on their own. Children are always someone's." As the cat strikes a confident, muscle-flexing pose, it interjects with a distinct voice, "Why doesn't it happen? I, for example, am a cat on my own. My own." The dog then chimes in with its own voice, "And I'm my own!", prompting the man to ask, "And you, actually, why are you interested? You're not from the police?" The cheerful music continues throughout this lively exchange in the cozy, lived-in room.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with a man, identified by his voice as Pechkin, the local postman, peeking through a wooden house window. He wears a brown hat and yellow coat, smiling and waving cheerfully as he states, "No, I'm not from the police. I'm from the post office. I'm the local postman. Pechkin. That's why I need to know everything to deliver the mail," before asking what they will subscribe to. He then pulls his hat off, waves again, and disappears. Inside, a red-haired boy, holding a long-handled duster, stands beside a brown dog, both looking toward the window. The boy's voice is heard saying he will subscribe to "Murzilka," while other male voices discuss subscribing to hunting magazines or saving money. A gray striped cat, initially hiding behind a green curtain, emerges and strikes a confident pose with a mischievous grin. The scene transitions to a cozy dining area, featuring a patchwork curtain, a small TV, and a wooden fireplace, where the boy, dog, and cat are seated at a wooden table with a golden samovar, blue teacups, and a plate of bread. Upbeat, whimsical background music begins as a male voice ponders, "Why are we always without milk, without milk? One could die like that. We should buy a cow." Another male voice agrees, but the boy's voice asks, "But where to get the money?" The conversation continues among male voices, debating borrowing from neighbors, how to pay it back (with milk), and then the logic of buying a cow if the milk is given away, concluding that they should sell something unnecessary. Amidst this discussion, the cat playfully interacts with the dog, who is eating from a bowl, while the boy sips tea and smiles. The cat then hides behind the green curtain again, reappears, and continues to engage with the surprised dog. The boy watches with amusement, holding his teacup and smiling contentedly as the whimsical music plays out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a dark, rocky cave, illuminated by a faint blue light, where the red-haired boy, the gray striped cat, and the brown dog are seen pulling a wooden chest on wheels, each holding a shovel. A triumphant male voice shouts "Sklad!" followed by a whoop. Suddenly, a black bird, identified as a jackdaw by its distinct squawks and flapping sounds, flies in, startling them. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a yellow coat, emerges from the shadows, dramatically gesturing as if to stop them. He confronts the group, sternly shouting "Отдай!" (Give it back!) twice, claiming the jackdaw stole his "Olympic ruble" and needs to be taken to a clinic for "experiments." The red-haired boy, speaking with a protesting voice, argues against this, stating they will cure the bird and teach it to talk, and explains they were "mushroom picking" with the chest. The man, initially stern, shifts to a surprised expression as the boy hands him the bird, then smiles warmly, takes the bird, and gently places it on his shoulder. The boy, cat (who lets out a distinct meow), and dog cheer joyfully, their shovels dropping. The scene transitions to a brightly lit kitchen with red cabinets and yellow tiled walls, where the red-haired woman, speaking with a scolding female voice, is drying dishes at a table, telling the red-haired man, who enters holding a white cloth and begins washing dishes at the sink, that "It's your fault" and he "spoiled" someone. The red-haired man, in turn, defends the person, stating in a male voice that "He just loves animals" and "That's why he left with the cat." The kitchen, adorned with hanging wooden spoons and utensils, presents a warm, domestic atmosphere, sharply contrasting with the earlier cave setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a brightly lit kitchen with red cabinets and yellow tiled walls, where an upbeat, whimsical instrumental tune plays. A red-haired woman in a pink blouse with a blue bow and glasses is drying a blue-rimmed plate with a yellow cloth, gesturing toward a red-haired man in a green sweater with a white pattern who is washing dishes at the sink. The man, speaking in a male voice, suggests, "We need to have dogs and cats in the house, and a whole bag of friends, and all sorts of hide-and-seek games. Then children won't disappear." The woman, in a female voice, retorts, "Then the parents will start disappearing. Because I'm already tired at work. I barely have enough strength to watch TV. And don't tell me your nonsense! You'd better tell me how we can find the boy?" The man turns to face her, holding a white cloth, and places a clean plate on a stack on the white tablecloth-covered table, continuing, "We need to print a notice in the newspaper. That a boy is missing. His name is Uncle Fyodor, height one meter twenty. If anyone sees him, let them inform us." The scene then cuts to a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and a window showing a green landscape. A gray striped cat with a white muzzle sits thoughtfully on the floor, then leaps onto a wooden bench by the window, watching a black bird with a yellow beak perched outside. A clock ticking sound is heard, followed by a bell. A brown dog with floppy ears and a white muzzle enters through a wooden door, looking around curiously. An off-screen male voice asks, "Oh, why are we feeding him for nothing? Let him be useful. Well, tell me, who's there? Who's there? Well, tell me, who's there? Who's there? Who's there?" as the dog stands on a striped rug, gesturing with its paws as if speaking, while the cat remains focused on the bird outside the window. The off-screen voice continues, "Ugh, don't you have anything to do? You'd better sing him songs..."

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a yellow coat, identified as Postman Pechkin, approaching a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof, carrying a white box. As he approaches, a high-pitched, repeated voice asks "Кто там?" (Who's there?). The camera pans to the roof, then cuts to a black bird with a yellow beak peeking nervously from a window with pink floral curtains, possibly making the "Кто там?" sound. Postman Pechkin knocks on the door, announcing, "Это я, почтальон Печкин, принёс заметку про вашего мальчика!" (It's me, Postman Pechkin, I brought a note about your boy!), but suddenly collapses to the ground with a thud, still holding the box, accompanied by background music. A red-haired boy, addressed as Uncle Fyodor, in a yellow shirt and blue pants, accompanied by a brown dog, rushes to the scene. The boy picks up the box, revealing a newspaper with a missing child ad in Cyrillic, which is then read aloud: "Пропал мальчик. Глаза голубые, рост метр двадцать, родители его ищут. Нашедшего ждёт премия - велосипед." (Boy missing. Blue eyes, height 1 meter 20, parents are looking for him. A reward awaits the finder - a bicycle.) The boy looks shocked. A gray striped cat with a white muzzle and chest, holding a stick, appears and gestures excitedly. The brown dog then speaks, comforting the boy, "Не горюй, дядь Фёдор." (Don't worry, Uncle Fyodor.), followed by the cat adding, "Да мало ли таких мальчиков. Может, и немало." (There are so many boys like that. Maybe not so many.) Postman Pechkin then reappears, standing by the door, pointing upward with a serious expression, seemingly addressing the boy or the cat.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and yellow coat, standing outside a rustic wooden house. He holds a long yellow measuring tape, appearing to measure the door, and is heard stating in Russian, "But bicycles are not given to everyone. Now I will measure your boy." The scene cuts to a view through a window with pink floral curtains, revealing a white cow with brown spots walking past a clothesline in a sunny yard. Inside, a red-haired boy in a yellow shirt with a blue stripe and blue pants sits at a wooden table, writing on a large sheet of paper with a red pen. A white envelope and a small lamp are beside him. He looks up, startled, as the cow is seen outside again, now with a small black bird perched on its back, accompanied by the sound of a bicycle bell ringing. The boy, heard saying, "My dad and mom. I live well. Simply wonderful. I have everything. Oh!", rushes to the window. Upbeat, accordion-driven folk music begins to play as he returns to his seat, only to be replaced by a gray striped cat, who sits at the table and begins writing on the same paper. The cat is then replaced by a brown dog, who also writes on the paper. The scene shifts to a cozy living room with red curtains, where the folk music continues. A red-haired woman in a pink blouse and glasses sits on a green couch, talking to a red-haired man in a green sweater who is wrapped in a black and white striped blanket. The woman gestures toward a framed still-life painting above the couch, which depicts a dark book, oranges, and a blue mug. She then looks thoughtful, and a thought bubble appears above her head, showing a small red-haired boy looking out of a pink house window, suggesting she is imagining her son in a cozy home. Throughout this latter part, the red-haired boy's voice is heard in a voiceover, repeating, "My dad and mom. I live well. Simply wonderful. I have everything. I have my own home."

segments.integrated_caption: In a cozy living room with red curtains, a red-haired man in a green sweater sits beside a red-haired woman in a pink blouse on a green couch. The man initially holds a piece of paper, pointing to a golden-framed still-life painting on the wall. The woman, wearing glasses, reacts with surprise as a thought bubble appears above her head, showing a small red-haired boy clutching a white cloth. The man then gestures toward the painting, and the thought bubble changes to show the boy now smiling joyfully in a red knitted sweater. Gentle, melancholic accordion music plays as the woman takes the paper and begins reading aloud in Russian. Her expression shifts from concern to relief as she reads a letter, seemingly from their 'son, Uncle Sharik,' detailing his health, mentioning 'paws ache' and 'tail falls off,' but then happily reporting he has started shedding, with 'old fur falling off' and 'new, clean, silky' fur growing, so his 'shagginess has increased.' The man, now reclining on the couch under a black and white striped blanket, sips from a small white cup, appearing content as he listens attentively, occasionally glancing at the painting. The woman finishes reading the letter, which concludes with 'Goodbye. Your son, Uncle Sharik.' The man then asks, 'What has increased for him?' to which the woman replies, 'Shagginess,' reinforcing the warm, intimate family moment centered around the well-being of their beloved 'Uncle Sharik,' with the painting serving as a backdrop for their shared emotions.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a warmly lit living room with red curtains and a green couch, where a red-haired woman in a pink blouse and blue skirt, wearing glasses, appears distressed, gesturing with her hands as she speaks in Russian, wondering if they've gone crazy or if their 'shagginess' has increased, allowing them to sleep in the snow. A red-haired man in a green sweater with a white pattern listens, then places a comforting hand on her shoulder, logically countering in Russian that people go crazy individually, unlike getting the flu together. Their interaction is interrupted by the cheerful entrance of a man with a mustache, wearing a yellow coat and brown hat, who waves and announces himself in Russian as 'Postman Pechkin from Prostokvashino,' asking if they are the ones giving away bicycles for a boy. Gentle, melancholic instrumental music begins as the scene cuts to a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and pink floral curtains framing a window, where a storm rages outside. Inside, a red-haired boy lies in bed, sick, with a thermometer in his mouth and a yellow scarf around his neck. A brown dog and a gray striped cat sit beside him, looking concerned. The music continues as the dog takes the thermometer, examines it, and reacts with alarm, dramatically gesturing and scolding the cat in Russian, blaming him for the boy's illness by giving him cold milk and even boasting about it. The cat defensively questions why he is being blamed. A knocking sound is heard, followed by a voice from outside asking 'Who's there?', to which a voice from inside replies 'It's us!', and the outside voice questions 'Our own?'. The final shot shows the closed wooden door of the room, with a red-handled latch, suggesting the characters are now isolated inside as the storm continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a dimly lit, cozy wooden room where a brown dog with floppy ears and a gray striped cat with green eyes sit on a dark surface, looking concerned. A deep, gruff male voice states, "In such weather, their own people sit at home, watch TV. Only strangers wander around. We won't open the door." The cat gestures animatedly, then raises its paw as if to speak. The camera shifts to a red-haired boy, appearing unwell, lying in a bed with a white metal headboard, covered by a dark blanket, wearing a white shirt and a yellow scarf. Beside him is a nightstand with a green bottle and a box of medicine, with a patchwork curtain to the right and a white curtain with colorful patches to the left. A child's urgent voice calls out, "Open up! It's my dad and mom who arrived." A door creaks open, and whimsical, accordion-led instrumental music begins as a man with a mustache, wearing a yellow coat and a brown hat, enters with a red bicycle, followed by a red-haired woman in a green coat and glasses. A theatrical, slightly sinister voice-over comments, "See what they've done to your child. They need to be immediately handed over to the clinic, and for experiments." The woman approaches the boy, gently touching his forehead and hand with concern. The boy then sits up, smiling, as the cat and dog sit beside him. The woman asks, "Where is your raspberry?" and the man replies, "Here it is, please." The cat, now holding a red jar, offers it to the boy, who takes it with a smile. The woman, surprised, exclaims, "Oh, I didn't even know cats could be so smart. I thought they only knew how to shout in trees." The man retorts, "Big deal. I can even embroider, and on a typewriter too," as the woman pets the cat affectionately. The scene transitions to a window with pink floral curtains, where a black bird with a yellow beak is perched, looking inside. The bird flaps its wings and hops on the windowsill, then flies away as the curtains flutter in the breeze, with the playful music continuing in the background, revealing a wooden fence and green trees under a cloudy sky outside.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens inside a rustic wooden room with a patchwork curtain, accompanied by upbeat, folksy music. A red-haired boy lies unwell under a dark blanket on a bed. A red-haired woman in a green coat and glasses stands beside him, gently touching his forehead and hand, telling him in a caring tone, "You, son, however you want, but we'll take you to the city. You need care." A red-haired man with a long nose and brown coat leans over a wooden stool, smiling warmly, adding encouragingly, "And if you want to take the cat or Sharik, or someone else? Take them!" The woman agrees, "We won't object." The boy sits up, smiling as he receives a red jar from a gray striped cat, named Matroskin by the man who says, "Well, Matroskin, you'll come with me." The cat, with a happy expression, sits beside the bed. The brown dog, named Sharik by the woman, sits nearby, looking on, and then speaks in a calm, reasonable voice, "We'll stay. You'd better come to us yourself. For holidays. And visit on weekends." The man then exclaims, "I'm off, Kabardin, damn it," and expresses concern for his farm, "And my cow, the farm, supplies for winter." The scene transitions to the floor, where the gray striped cat and brown dog sit on a striped rug, gesturing and interacting playfully. The setting then shifts outdoors to a rural yard with a blue car parked near a wooden fence. The red-haired woman, boy, and dog stand by the car as the man drives, holding a small black bird with a yellow beak. The man urgently calls out, "Stop! Stop!" before offering, "Here, take it. You'll have more fun with him." The woman takes the bird, cradling it gently, while the cat and dog look on with delight. The bird flaps its wings and hops, and the woman smiles, holding it close as the boy and dog watch with joy. The woman then asks, "Who's there?" as a new voice, belonging to postman Pechkin, announces, "It's me, postman Pechkin, I brought the Murzilka magazine." The woman responds with an embarrassed, "Oh, how awkward," to which Pechkin slightly reproaches, "You completely forgot about Pechkin." The atmosphere throughout is warm and affectionate, emphasizing family and animal companionship in a cozy, rural setting, all underscored by the continuous upbeat, folksy music.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with an upbeat, folksy instrumental tune as the man with the mustache and brown hat, wearing a yellow coat, stands beside a red bicycle with white handlebars in a rural setting. He gestures cheerfully, then mounts the bicycle, smiling broadly. A male voice, presumably the man with the mustache, comments, "And rightly so. He's so harmful." He then apologetically asks, "Excuse me. Why was I harmful? Because I didn't have a bicycle." He continues, "And now I'll immediately start to become kinder. And I'll get some kind of little beast. So life is merrier. You come home, and it's happy to see you." The scene cuts to the red-haired woman with glasses and a green coat, accompanied by the brown dog and gray striped cat, who wave happily. The man reappears on the bicycle, now with the dog and cat riding with him, all waving joyfully, accompanied by the sound of a bicycle bell. A male voice invites, "Come visit us in Prostokvashino!" The scene transitions to a blue car driving past a signpost reading 'ПРОСТОКВАШИНО 100 м' in black and red Cyrillic text, indicating the village's proximity. The man with the mustache is then shown riding the bicycle with the dog and cat, all waving as they move along a dirt path, with the bicycle bell ringing again. The video concludes with a black screen displaying 'КОНЕЦ' in bold yellow Cyrillic text, followed by the 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' logo and the title card 'КАНИКУЛЫ В ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' in white Cyrillic text on a blue background, all set to the continuing cheerful instrumental music, followed by credits listing the scriptwriter, director, composer, and other crew members in white Cyrillic text on the same blue background.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with production credits in white Cyrillic text on a solid blue background, listing artists, voice actors, assistants, and the film director, concluding with a 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ, 1980г.' copyright notice, all set to lively, upbeat, traditional-sounding instrumental music featuring accordion, strings, and percussion. The scene then transitions to a sunny rural yard with a wooden fence, green trees, and a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof. A white cow with brown spots walks along a dirt path toward the house, passing a clothesline with laundry. Inside the cozy wooden room, a gray striped cat sits on a wooden stool beside a table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, pouring a drink from a brown jug into a white mug. A brown dog with floppy ears sits on a wooden chair across the table, looking sad and thoughtful. As the cat sips from the mug, the dog’s expression shifts to surprise and then to a happy, smiling look, watching the cat. The cat, with a cheerful expression, looks at the dog, who responds with a joyful, open-mouthed smile, as the background music continues. The dog then speaks in a sad, thoughtful male voice, saying, "And I say, drink. There's nowhere to put your milk. All the buckets, all the basins are full of milk. Even the washbasin has milk." The cat, in a somewhat sarcastic and dismissive male voice, replies, "So what? I'll get a second cow then." The dog, concerned, asks, "And how will you wash?" The cat dismissively answers, "Like that." The dog, confused, asks, "How?" to which the cat sarcastically retorts, "You need to get less dirty. And anyway, some people wash with their tongue," eliciting an exasperated "Oh!" from the dog.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a cozy wooden room with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. A gray striped cat, speaking with a distinct male voice, sits on a wooden stool, pouring a drink from a brown jug into a white mug, remarking, "And some even eat mice," before sighing and playfully suggesting, "And if your cow were smarter, it wouldn't give milk, but carbonated water. Pepsi-Cola, for example." Across the table, a brown dog, initially looking sad, shifts to a surprised and joyful expression, excitedly interjecting, "Oh, or kvass!" The cat then teases, "And if you were smarter, you would have started working a long time ago." The dog, with an eager male voice, declares, "And I will. I'm going hunting now," prompting a sigh from the cat, who then asks, "And aren't you sorry to shoot little animals?" The dog replies, "Sorry," but explains, "Well, if an instinct awakens in me, I'm drawn to the forest with terrible force." The cat reacts with exasperated sounds and a chuckle, ironically stating, "And animals, by the way, are created for that, to be hunted." The dog, excited, promises, "I'll be back soon, I'll shoot something tasty," and exclaims, "Oh, if only Uncle Fyodor saw me!" The cat dismisses him with a playful insult, "Go, go, you unfortunate spaniel." A gentle, melancholic piano melody then begins to play as the scene cuts to a different room with red floral wallpaper. Here, a red-haired man reclines on a green couch under a black and white striped blanket, reading a newspaper, while beside him, a red-haired boy with glasses sits, reading a book with large Cyrillic letters. A small TV and a framed still-life painting are visible, and a poster above the TV shows a drawing of the gray striped cat and brown dog, labeled 'МАТРОСКИН ШАРИК' (Matroskin Sharik). The boy points to the letter 'Ж' in his book, then looks up at the poster, seemingly connecting the characters in the book to the poster, shifting the atmosphere from playful domesticity to a quiet, studious moment, suggesting a narrative link between the characters in the two scenes.

segments.integrated_caption: In a cozy living room with red floral wallpaper, an upbeat, whimsical tune plays as a red-haired man, identified as 'папа' (dad), reclines on a green couch, reading a newspaper. Beside him, a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater sits on a black-and-white striped blanket, engrossed in a large book with Cyrillic letters 'ЖЗ'. The boy repeatedly sounds out 'Кар!' as he learns letters, encouraged by the man who prompts him to 'crow this letter'. A poster of a gray striped cat and a brown dog labeled 'МАТРОСКИН ШАРИК' hangs on the wall. The scene shifts to a kitchen where a red-haired woman with glasses, wearing a pink turtleneck, initially joins the boy's letter game with a playful 'Ну, а это-то какая?', but quickly becomes exasperated, declaring 'Хватит! Хватит, надоело!' She then dramatically complains about her 'hopeless' life, feeling like a 'serf peasant' with 'four evening silk dresses' but 'nowhere to wear them'. With a determined tone, she announces, 'We're leaving for the resort tomorrow!' The man calmly questions her, suggesting their 'wonderful house' in 'Prostokvashino' is sufficient. A doorbell rings, prompting the boy to excitedly ask 'Who's there?', to which the woman sarcastically replies, 'Nobody came. Our dad has gone crazy. He won't let us go to the resort.' The boy then aligns with his father, stating, 'I don't want to go to the resort either. I want to go to Prostokvashino!' The woman, now by a wooden wardrobe, pulls out a green suitcase and begins packing, vehemently arguing against Prostokvashino, asking what she would do there, and declaring, 'I've been working with a frying pan for you all year. And now I want to go to the resort!' The scene cuts back to the living room as the man gestures toward the boy, who looks up from his book, while the woman continues packing a blue dress, her focused actions contrasting the man's relaxed demeanor and the boy's quiet reading, all set against the backdrop of their domestic setting with a small TV and framed pictures.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens at a bustling train station, where a red-haired woman in a blue suit and glasses, a bearded man in a green jacket, and their red-haired son, Fyodor, in a yellow sweater, walk along the platform with luggage. A station announcement, "Внимание, внимание! Поезд Москва-Сочи отходит с четвёртого пути. Электричка на Простоквашино с пятого," clarifies the signs for 'СОЧИ' and 'ПРОС' (Prostokvashino) as a digital clock ticks from 10:42 to 10:43. The family approaches the red 'МОСКВА-СОЧИ' train, but Fyodor, carrying a stick with a black bird, confidently boards the green 'МП' train to Prostokvashino, exclaiming, "Мама! Мама! Ты за меня не беспокойся! Я с Матроскиным и Шариком не пропаду, мы горошком держим!" The woman, frustrated, cries out, "Послушай! А где же наш мальчик? Куда пропал дядя Фёдор? Я тебя воспитывала! Я из-за тебя ночей не спала! А ты... на электричке едешь!" The man, however, calmly lights his pipe with a match, exhaling smoke thoughtfully as he states, "Он правильно едет. Ему в деревне лучше. У него же нет вечерних платьев." The scene then shifts to a serene rural landscape with a river, green trees, and rolling hills, where the black bird flies across the frame, followed by a white horse pulling a wooden cart loaded with boxes along the riverbank, all accompanied by cheerful, folksy music.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens at a bustling train station, where a red-haired woman in a blue suit and glasses, a bearded man in a green jacket, and a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater and blue pants are seen walking along the platform with luggage. The station displays signs for 'СОЧИ' (Sochi) and 'ПРОС' (likely 'Простоквашино'), with a digital clock showing 10:42, which ticks to 10:43. The family approaches a red train marked 'МОСКВА-СОЧИ' (Moscow-Sochi), and the boy, carrying a stick with a black bird perched on it, boards a green train labeled 'МП' with a hammer and sickle emblem. The woman and man stop, looking confused as the boy disappears into the green train. The woman gestures in frustration, and the man pulls out a pipe, lighting it with a match, then exhaling smoke in a thoughtful manner. The scene abruptly shifts to a serene rural landscape with a river, green trees, and rolling hills, where a small black bird flies across the frame, followed by a white horse pulling a cart loaded with boxes, moving along the riverbank.

The video begins underwater in a green-hued aquatic environment, where a brown dog, dressed as a cowboy with a green hat, bandolier, and rifle, dangles upside down, exhaling bubbles as he struggles. A male voice urges him, "Эй, дурень, бросай ружьё, да всплывай поскорей." (Hey, fool, drop the rifle, and surface quickly.) The dog, with a struggling, higher-pitched voice, replies, "Да я бы рад бросить, да Матроскин мне голову оторвёт. Без ружья-то деньги плаченые? А моя жизнь бесплатная?" (I'd be glad to drop it, but Matroskin will tear my head off. Without the rifle, are the payments made? And my life is free?) He then declares, "Всё. Считайте, что я утонул." (That's it. Consider me drowned.) A red beaver with a mischievous expression swims toward him, causing the dog to react with surprise and fear while still clutching his rifle. The scene abruptly shifts to a rustic wooden room with a whimsical background tune, where a gray striped cat is seated at a table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, operating a black sewing machine that whirs distinctly. A black bird with a yellow beak flies in through the window, perches on the sill, chirping, and then lands on the table. After a knocking sound, the cat asks, "Кто там?" (Who's there?) The bird, speaking in a male voice, identifies himself as "почтальон Печкин" (Postman Pechkin) and says he brought "заметку про вашего мальчика" (a note about your boy). The cat replies that they don't need a note about a boy, as they don't have one, and "Он у нас в городе живёт." (He lives in the city.) The bird then hops onto the cat’s back, making a "Кар-кар" sound and exclaiming "Ура!" (Hooray!), prompting the cat to react with surprise, laugh, and toss the bird aside. The cat then stands up, waving cheerfully, as the bird flies away, leaving the cat smiling contentedly.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a rustic wooden house with a striped rug at the doorway, as a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater and blue pants enters, carrying a green backpack. An excited male voice, belonging to a red beaver, exclaims, "Ah, finally, my beloved Uncle Fyodor has arrived! Now we'll save twice as much hay for our cow!" A gray striped cat with white stripes and a white muzzle greets the boy excitedly, jumping up and down. A small black bird with a yellow beak flies in through the open door and lands on the cat’s back, causing the cat to react with surprise. The boy waves goodbye as he steps outside, and the scene shifts to the yard where a brown dog with floppy ears and a white muzzle, dressed in a gray shirt, is being carried on the back of the red beaver, who holds a rifle and appears mischievous. The dog, identified by the beaver as "Sharik," is asked about by another male voice, to which the beaver responds, "Ah, there he is! Returning from the hunt. A little hunter. Probably dragging prey." Upbeat, mischievous instrumental music begins as the beaver walks past the house. The dog looks distressed, complaining in a male voice, "I have nothing to do. Just pulling different dogs out of the river. And I didn't ask to be pulled out. I, I might not have been drowning at all. Or maybe I was just scuba diving!" A "thud" is heard, and the beaver exclaims, "Here you go! For our Sharik!" The dog then looks relieved as the beaver stops. The boy returns, and the beaver and dog stand before him, the dog now looking happy. The music fades as the scene transitions to the interior of the house, where the boy, cat, and dog are seated at a wooden table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. A large golden samovar with a white teapot on top serves as the centerpiece, and the boy sips from a blue teacup while the cat and dog look on. The cat, now sitting on a wooden stool, pours a drink from a brown jug into a white mug with a gold rim, then drinks from it. The dog, seated on a wooden chair with a yellow backrest, watches with a smile. The room features a patchwork curtain, a wooden fireplace, and a framed still-life painting above the couch, creating a warm and cozy atmosphere as the characters enjoy a peaceful tea time together. The beaver's voice is heard sighing, "Oh, this Sharik..."

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a cozy wooden room with rustic charm, where a gray striped cat, a brown dog, and a red-haired boy are seated around a wooden table covered with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. A large golden samovar with a white teapot sits at the center, flanked by blue teacups and a plate of bread. Whimsical, slightly melancholic music plays in the background. The gray striped cat, wearing a white scarf, animatedly gestures and speaks in a higher-pitched voice, pondering the dog's lack of hunting success, stating, "Ah, a hunter found. No income from you, only expenses," and then questioning, "What to do with him? He'll perish without hunting, shrivel up completely." As the cat pours a drink from the samovar into a white mug, it continues, suggesting, "Or maybe we should make him a sled dog, huh? We'll buy him a cart, we'll carry milk to the market on him. We'll cultivate the garden on him." The brown dog, named "Sharik" by the cat, sits on a wooden chair with a yellow backrest, watching with a mix of curiosity and concern, occasionally resting his chin on his paw. He responds in a deeper, melancholic voice, expressing resignation, "Ah, do whatever you want. Even a scarecrow. Life is not dear to me anyway." The red-haired boy, in his yellow sweater and blue pants, sips from his cup, listening intently and reacting with subtle expressions to the conversation. The cat takes a bite of bread, and the dog looks on thoughtfully. The cat then declares, "No, no, no, I've decided. I'll write a letter to mom and dad. Let them send a photo-gun for Sharik. That's hunting, and no need to kill animals." The background features a patchwork curtain, a wooden wall, and a yellow guitar. The music shifts to a more upbeat and adventurous tone as the scene abruptly transitions to a bright beach setting. Two striped deck chairs sit on golden sand under a radiant sun, with a sailboat visible on the blue ocean. The chairs are occupied by two figures, one holding a drink, both relaxing under palm fronds, suggesting a peaceful, sunny day by the sea.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a serene beach with a bright yellow sun, where a red-haired woman in a blue swimsuit and a shirtless man with a beard lounge in striped deck chairs, gesturing as they converse in Russian. The man suggests leaving their resort for "Prostokvashino," expressing concern for a boy, but the woman, speaking practically, dismisses his worries, highlighting the boy's well-being and his protective gray striped cat, leading the man to wistfully remark about having such a cat himself. The scene then cuts to a dimly lit wooden room at night, where a crescent moon is visible through a window. The gray striped cat sleeps atop a blue chest, and a red-haired boy sleeps under a patchwork quilt, while a black bird perches on a wardrobe. A melancholic brass instrument plays, transitioning to an upbeat brass band tune as curtains flutter and a lantern's light appears outside. A wooden door creaks open, and a brown dog enters, holding the lantern, identified by a boisterous male voice as "Postman Sharik, I mean, just Sharik!" The red-haired boy, now awake, invites him in with a child's voice. The scene shifts to a barn at night, where Sharik, still holding the lantern, illuminates the red-haired boy, the gray striped cat, a white cow with brown spots, and a small brown calf. Sharik excitedly announces in Russian that "Our cow has given birth to a calf!", prompting a cow's moo and a deeper male voice to express satisfaction about the cow, as the gray striped cat gestures excitedly and the red-haired boy looks on with a hand to his face, appearing surprised or concerned.

segments.integrated_caption: In a dimly lit barn at night, illuminated by a lantern, the brown dog, named Sharik, and the red-haired boy, addressed as Uncle Fyodor, stand together, engaged in a lively debate with the animated gray striped cat, Matroskin. Sharik, initially calm, gestures with his paw while speaking in a male voice, suggesting they take 'him' (referring to the calf) home. Matroskin, expressive and gesturing excitedly with both paws, responds in a distinct male voice, agreeing to take 'mom' (the cow) home too, but then emphatically argues that the white cow with brown spots is 'state-owned' because they rented her, and therefore the small brown calf, who stands beside her looking attentive and slightly nervous, is also 'state-owned.' Uncle Fyodor, listening attentively with a hand to his face, interjects in a slightly deeper male voice, telling Sharik to be quiet, and counters that while the cow is state-owned, everything she produces, like milk or calves, is theirs. Matroskin then uses an analogy of a rented refrigerator and its 'frost' to press his point, while Sharik argues they only rented one cow but now have two. Matroskin, pointing toward the cow and calf, insists they must adhere to receipts and reporting, stating they will only return one cow. An off-screen female voice then questions their argument, reminding Matroskin that he intended to buy the cow permanently and suggesting he should take both the cow and calf. Matroskin begins to respond as the segment ends, all within the rustic, enclosed atmosphere of the barn with its wooden walls and hay-covered floor, the lantern casting soft shadows on the group as they engage in this humorous dispute over ownership.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens outside a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a red roof, where a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands holding a yellow-framed object. He gestures animatedly while speaking to a red-haired boy in a yellow sweater and blue pants, who listens with a hand on his hip. The man, in an energetic voice, declares he has brought a parcel but won't give it to them because they lack documents, adding that "tailed ones aren't supposed to have documents at all." The boy indignantly asks why he brought it then, to which the man explains it's "how it's supposed to be" but without documents, it can't be given away, threatening to "walk around like this for a whole week." The boy demands the parcel, prompting the man to ask about their documents. Beside them, a brown dog with floppy ears and a gray striped cat with white markings stand close, the dog looking concerned and the cat gesturing excitedly. The cat, in a high-pitched voice, exclaims "Whiskers and a tail!" as its documents, followed by the dog, in a deeper, slightly gruff voice, presenting "Well, here are my documents!" The man counters, asking if they have a "stamp on their tail," to which the cat sadly replies "No," and the man dismisses whiskers as faked. A "boing" sound effect punctuates the exchange. The man raises the yellow frame to his face as if taking a picture, then the scene cuts to a night sky with a crescent moon and stars above the house’s roof, accompanied by gentle, melancholic music. A sigh and a "whoosh" sound are heard. The next shot shows the boy, dog, and cat standing together on the porch steps, looking up thoughtfully, followed by another sigh. The dog then sits on the step, crossing his arms, while the cat sits beside him, both appearing contemplative. The man's voice returns, calmer, reflecting, "This is probably my photo-gun that arrived." The dog shifts his posture, looking down with a worried expression, and the cat raises a paw as if making a point, suggesting a moment of reflection or decision after the man’s departure, as the melancholic music continues to play.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens outside a rustic wooden house with blue shutters, where a brown dog and a gray striped cat stand together. The cat, gesturing excitedly, speaks in a high-pitched, animated voice, proposing, "Я вот что придумал! Мы такой же ящик найдем, как у Печкина! И когда он снова придет... Да ящики ему поменяем!" (I've come up with something! We'll find the same kind of box as Pechkin's! And when he comes again... We'll swap his boxes!). The red-haired boy appears, standing by the house with a hand on his hip, listening intently and responding with a questioning "Что?" (What?) before exclaiming "Ура!" (Hooray!) at the cat's plan. The dog looks concerned, then the boy calls out, "Гаврюша! Ко мне! Дай лапу!" (Gavryusha! Come here! Give me your paw!), to which the dog barks. The boy gestures toward the window, where a dark shape is visible inside, while the cat raises a paw as if making a point, and the dog sits on the step with arms crossed. The boy stands on the porch steps with the dog and cat, looking thoughtful. Suddenly, a white cow with brown spots appears, looking at the group with curiosity. Upbeat, playful music begins as the camera zooms out to show the entire house, with the cat peeking from the attic window, then climbing out and waving. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, arrives holding a yellow-framed object resembling a camera or projector. He approaches the door, and the cat peeks from the window, then opens the door and greets him with a wave. The man, identifying himself as "почтальон Печкин" (Postman Pechkin), states, "принес посылку. Только я вам ее не отдам, потому что у вас документов нету." (I brought a parcel. But I won't give it to you, because you don't have documents.). The cat, stepping out and waving cheerfully, retorts, "А мы бы эту посылку и сами у вас не взяли. Зачем это нам гуталин?" (And we wouldn't have taken this parcel from you ourselves. Why would we need shoe polish?), to which the confused postman asks, "Какой такой гуталин?" (What kind of shoe polish?), and the cat explains, "Обыкновенный, которым ботинки чистят." (Ordinary, the kind you clean shoes with.). The man raises the yellow-framed object to his face as if taking a picture, accompanied by a camera click sound. He then turns and walks away, and the cat follows him, waving goodbye. The final shot shows the cat standing in the doorway, waving with a happy expression, as the playful music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens inside a rustic wooden room, filled with upbeat, folksy instrumental music. A gray striped cat, named Vasya, sits beside a man with a mustache, brown hat, and long yellow coat, who animatedly gestures while speaking in a male voice, suggesting, "Give her not those candies. They are very wonderful." A golden samovar with a white teapot sits on the red-and-white checkered tablecloth, and Vasya reaches for a blue teacup. The man raises a yellow-framed object, resembling a camera, to his face as if taking a picture, then walks out the wooden door, followed by Vasya. The cat's male voice exclaims, "Give it back, give it back! This is my candy!" as the man re-enters, carrying a large yellow wooden box labeled in Cyrillic for 'Uncle Fyodor' and 'Sharik' from 'Dad and Mom' at a 'Black Sea Resort'. The man, identified as Uncle Fyodor, places the box on the table, remarking in his male voice, "Vasya, to what lengths people go, greedy for someone else's good!" Vasya excitedly jumps to inspect the box. A red-haired woman in a yellow sweater and black turtleneck enters, smiling as she reads the label. A brown dog with floppy ears, named Sharik, sits on a wooden chair, holding a black camera and pointing it at the box. The woman then pulls out a piece of paper and reads aloud in her female voice, "Well, Sharik, now you will be engaged in photo hunting. You will photograph animals and send photos to various magazines." Sharik, the dog, responds pragmatically in his male voice, "Right, where they pay more." The woman continues reading the letter, her expression shifting to delight, "Don't be sad, Uncle Fyodor. Hold on, we'll be there soon. Only one dress left. Dad and Mom." The scene cuts to a black screen, then to a blue rabbit running through a grassy field, followed by Sharik the dog chasing it with his camera, suggesting a playful pursuit, all accompanied by the continuous lively music.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with a dynamic chase in a sunlit, grassy field, set to upbeat, fast-paced folk music with a strong drum beat and string instruments. A brown dog, clutching a black camera, runs frantically along a dirt path, pursuing a small blue rabbit. The dog's expression shifts from determined to startled as it fires its camera, causing a puff of smoke, while the rabbit continues to flee. The music continues as the scene transitions to a rural yard with wooden fences and a barn. Here, a red-haired woman in a yellow sweater and blue pants is sawing a log, accompanied by the distinct sound of sawing, with a gray striped cat assisting her. Suddenly, the dog bursts in, camera still in hand, and accidentally causes a large explosion from the log, startling the woman, who lets out a gasp, and the cat, which hisses in surprise. The music becomes more subdued as the woman, now holding the blue rabbit, looks annoyed, while the dog approaches sheepishly with a guilty expression. A male voice, later identified as the red-haired man, speaks in Russian with an exasperated tone, explaining he spent half the day chasing the rabbit to photograph it. The woman responds in Russian with a slightly sarcastic tone, suggesting he'll now have to chase the dog to get the photo back. The scene then shifts to a wider view of the yard as the red-haired man, wearing a green coat and brown pants, arrives with a green plaid suitcase. The music dramatically shifts to a joyful, celebratory fanfare with brass and strings, as the woman joyfully embraces the man, lifting the cat into the air. A chorus of voices shouts "Hooray!" in Russian, expressing extreme joy. The dog looks on with a mix of sadness and longing before the man pets it, and the group stands together in a warm, familial reunion against the rural landscape of green hills and wooden houses.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a sunny rural yard, framed by a wooden fence with green hills and wooden houses in the background, accompanied by upbeat, whimsical accordion music. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands near the fence, adjusting his hat and gesturing animatedly, exclaiming "Здравствуйте! С прибытием!" (Hello! Welcome!). A red-haired woman in a blue suit approaches him, greeting him warmly, "Здравствуйте, здравствуйте, дорогой Игорь Иванович!" (Hello, hello, dear Igor Ivanovich!), and they exchange a brief, friendly interaction, with the man playfully asking, "Не хотите, гражданка, целоваться?" (Don't you want to kiss, citizen?) and then stating, "Давайте сначала конфликт разберём" (Let's first sort out the conflict). When she asks "Какой такой конфликт?" (What kind of conflict?), he complains, "Посмотрите, что они с моей шапкой сделали!" (Look what they did to my hat!), suggesting "Через неё теперь хорошо вермишель отбрасывать" (Now it's good for draining vermicelli through it). The woman, addressing him as "товарищ Печкин" (Comrade Pechkin), tries to calm him, eventually offering him a new "головной убор" (headwear). The man then removes his hat, revealing a long black ponytail, and places a white sailor-style cap with the Cyrillic text 'ПОЧТА' (POST) on his head, remarking, "Бывает ты почта полевая, а у нас морская будет" (Sometimes you're field post, but ours will be naval). The woman, now holding a brown dog, is joined by a gray striped cat, and they all look on as the man salutes. The scene shifts to the man approaching a rustic wooden house with blue shutters, where he knocks on the window, offering, "А давайте, я вас на память из фоторужья щёлкну!" (And let me snap you for memory with a photo-gun!). Inside, a woman with dark hair and a white hat peers out, and the man gestures toward her. The dog, now holding a black camera, runs up and salutes the man, who returns the salute, while the man humorously declines being photographed himself, stating, "Не надо меня из ружья щёлкать! Я может только жить начинаю. На пенсию перехожу" (Don't snap me with a gun! I might just be starting to live. I'm retiring). The man then looks through the window again, smiling, as the woman inside waves back. The scene cuts to a birch forest with a yellow bird perched on a branch, chirping, as the camera pans upward to the sky, ending the sequence on a peaceful natural note, with the whimsical music fading out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat, folksy instrumental melody, featuring a balalaika-like sound, accompanying a vibrant, sunlit forest scene with birch trees and yellow birds, establishing a cheerful, pastoral atmosphere. The camera pans right to reveal a rustic wooden house with blue shutters and a wooden fence, where a man with a mustache and brown hat, carrying a white box with black squares, greets a red-haired woman in a blue suit who holds a brown dog. A white cow with brown spots stands calmly nearby. The music continues as the scene shifts to a field where a red-haired boy rides a small brown calf, while a man in a white shirt digs with a shovel, accompanied by a gray striped cat. The camera then focuses on the red-haired woman standing in a lush flower garden, holding a bouquet of white and blue flowers, with butterflies fluttering around her. She smiles and gestures warmly, and her voice is heard saying in Russian, "Yes. I understood a lot. And if I had a second life, I would spend it here. In Prostokvashino." The scene transitions to a bright blue sky with white clouds, where colorful Cyrillic text appears: 'ВСЕ САМЫЕ ЛЮБИМЫЕ МУЛЬТИКИ ИЗ ДЕТСТВА' (All the most beloved cartoons from childhood), followed by 'И многое, многое другое! Подписывайся!' (And much, much more! Subscribe!). An excited child's voice enthusiastically proclaims, "All the most beloved cartoons from childhood on the Soyuzmultfilm channel! Subscribe!" as the screen turns solid blue, displaying the 'СОЮЗМУЛЬТФИЛЬМ' logo in white, followed by the title 'ЗИМА В ПРОСТОКВАШИНО' (Winter in Prostokvashino) in bold white letters. The upbeat music fades, replaced by a distinct, melancholic instrumental piece, possibly featuring a guitar and harmonica, as the final shot reveals a serene, snow-covered village with wooden houses, snow-laden trees, and gentle snowfall, setting a peaceful winter mood.

segments.integrated_caption: Inside a rustic wooden room with log walls, a striped rug, and a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, a lively, whimsical folk tune plays. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). A small black bird with a yellow beak flutters erratically on the floor, startling the man who exclaims "Ну и ну!" (Well, well!) and "Что делается?" (What's going on?). A gray striped cat, wearing a red hat and scarf, runs around excitedly, then, after a brief moment of confusion, grabs the bird and tosses it aside, turning to the man with a mischievous expression. The man, looking surprised and slightly alarmed, gestures with his hand as he laments, "На дворе конец двадцатого века, а у нас в доме одна пара валенок на двоих. Ну, как при царе Горохе!" (It's the end of the 20th century outside, and in our house, there's one pair of felt boots for two. Like in the time of Tsar Gorokh!). He continues, asking, "А почему так вышло-то? Что у вас средств нету? То есть, денег у вас не хватает?" (But why did it turn out this way? Do you not have funds? That is, you don't have enough money?), to which a slightly different voice responds, "Средства у нас есть." (We have funds.). The man then declares, "У нас ума не хватает." (We lack intelligence/sense.), placing his postal bag on the floor and stepping back exasperatedly. The cat pulls at its red scarf, seemingly trying to adjust it, while the bird scurries away. The man raises his hand again, continuing his monologue, "Говорил я Тимохотнику: 'Купи себе валенки!' А он что?" (I told Timokhotnik: 'Buy yourself felt boots!' And what did he do?), with the other voice interjecting, "Что он?" (What did he do?). The man concludes, "Пошёл и кеды купил! Они говорят: 'Красивые!' И он, не подумавши, и сделал. У нас зимой национальная деревенская одежда какая? Валенки, штаны ватные, тулуп и шапка." (He went and bought sneakers! They say: 'They're beautiful!' And he did it without thinking. What is our national village clothing in winter? Felt boots, padded pants, a sheepskin coat, and a hat.). The scene, featuring a window with pink floral curtains and a view of green trees, a patchwork curtain, and a wooden door, maintains a chaotic yet humorous atmosphere with exaggerated expressions and movements.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens inside a cozy wooden room with rustic log walls, accompanied by whimsical, comedic background music. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands near the door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He gestures animatedly, speaking in a deep, expressive Russian voice, initially scolding someone as a 'blockhead' and urging them to 'open their eyes' to understand. A small black bird with a yellow beak flies in and perches on the window sill, then flutters near the man, but without making any distinct sounds. The man then turns to a gray striped cat, named Шарик, who is seated at a wooden table, wearing a red hat and scarf. The cat, with a higher-pitched, slightly whiny Russian voice, complains that they haven't spoken for 'two days,' which the man corrects to 'three.' The cat then suggests sending a telegram or letter, to which the man, holding his 'ПОЧТА' bag, explains the purpose of mail. He asks the cat if he wants a 'simple or congratulatory' form, and the cat, adjusting its scarf, insists on a 'simple' one, complaining about being spoiled and not needing anything from the man. As the cat begins writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper, the man explains he only has 'congratulatory' forms. The cat, looking annoyed, sighs about 'extending expenses,' but the man, now holding a long black pole, eventually concedes. The cat listens, then looks up with a mischievous grin, holding the pencil. The man then raises his hand, holding the black bag again, and in a cooing tone, calls the cat 'Шарик, you're a good boy,' only for the cat to immediately correct him, stating 'Incorrect.' The lively and humorous atmosphere is underscored by the exaggerated expressions and movements typical of classic animation, accompanied by the continuous whimsical music.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a cozy, dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and a red-and-white checkered tablecloth. A gray striped cat, wearing a red hat with a white pom-pom and a red scarf, sits at a wooden table, focused on writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper. A man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, enters through a wooden door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST), and his deep, theatrical voice states that if the form is congratulatory, the addressee must first be congratulated. The cat, with a higher-pitched, whiny, and sarcastic voice, responds with a theatrical 'I congratulate you, little ball!' followed by a 'boing' and 'thwack' sound. Suddenly, the camera cuts to a window where a brown dog, dressed in a blue shirt and a cowboy hat, peeks in with a surprised look, reacting with a gruff 'You idiot!' The cat then lets out a 'meow' before the man, gesturing animatedly, asks what to write next. The cat suggests writing about the weather, to which the dog, with a deep, gruff voice, declares the weather is good. The cat immediately retorts that it's not good, as a blizzard has been howling for two days, ruining all hunting. The man then scolds the dog, telling 'Comrade Dog' to be quiet and write about his own weather when he replies. The dog defiantly states he won't write a reply and threatens to throw a poker at the cat for calling names. The man questions why he would throw it if there's mail, then explains they will pack it and give it to the cat, as it's a parcel. He then informs the dog that a poker was sent to him as a parcel, as whimsical, accordion-like music begins to play, continuing as the man repeats his statement about the poker parcel.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a rustic wooden room where a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, stands near a door, holding a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He appears surprised and exasperated, exclaiming "Что-что-что?" (What-what-what?) and playfully threatening, "Да я занесу в него утюгом запустить!" (I'll launch an iron at him!). A gray striped cat, wearing a red hat with a white pom-pom and a red scarf, sits at a wooden table writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper, accompanied by a distinct writing sound. The man then calms, saying "Минуточку." (Just a minute.) and noting, "Больше килограмма." (More than a kilogram.), followed by a "thump" sound as he holds up a black object resembling a camera or projector, labeled 'ПОЧТА', and gestures excitedly, declaring, "Это посылка, получается. Сейчас мы её доставим." (This is a parcel, it turns out. Now we will deliver it.). He continues, "А если вы на него бочку вкатите? Это уже контейнерная перевозка получается. Этим транс-агентство занимается, сельской местности." (And if you roll a barrel onto it? That's already container shipping, it turns out. A transport agency deals with this, in rural areas.). An upbeat, whimsical instrumental tune with a strong bassline and percussion begins as the scene transitions to an exterior winter landscape, showing a snow-covered wooden house with a red chimney, surrounded by snow-laden trees and gentle snowfall. The camera zooms out to reveal a wider snow-covered village with multiple houses and bare trees, all blanketed in snow. The scene then shifts to a framed painting of the same house in summer, hanging on a red-striped wall in a cozy living room. The living room is filled with vibrant flowers, a blue toy car, which makes a "clunk" and then a "squeak" sound, elevated on a pot, a green couch, and a small TV. A red-haired man and a red-haired boy are seen interacting with the toy car, the boy looking up at the man with a curious expression. The red-haired woman enters the room, holding a potted plant, wearing a blue suit and sunglasses, and waving cheerfully, expressing her frustration with "Как мне всё это надоело!" (How tired I am of all this!). A calendar on the wall reads '30 ЯНВАРЯ' (January 30), as the music continues, transitioning the atmosphere from a cold, snowy exterior to a warm, colorful indoor setting, emphasizing seasonal contrast and domestic life.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with upbeat, whimsical background music as a red-haired woman, looking distressed and holding a wilting potted plant, enters the living room from the kitchen. She gestures in frustration, remarking in a slightly sarcastic tone, "Наша квартира мне телевизионную передачу напоминает. Что? Где? Когда? Называется." (Our apartment reminds me of a TV show. What? Where? When? It's called.) The calendar on the wall squeaks as it flips from '30 ДЕКАБРЯ' to '31 ДЕКАБРЯ'. Her expression shifts to exasperation as she adds, "А не поймёшь, что где валяется и когда всё это кончится," (And you won't understand what's lying where and when all this will end,) before placing the plant down with a clatter. The view cuts to a vibrant living room with red-striped walls and a green couch, where a red-haired man and a red-haired boy are playfully interacting with a blue toy car, which makes an engine sound as the man opens its hood, pretending to fix it while the boy watches amused. The man then picks up a black tire, holding it up to his face like a mask with a whimsical sound effect, playfully gesturing toward the boy, who laughs. The woman re-enters, now seated on a yellow armchair, and in a frustrated, accusatory tone, states, "От вашего бензина у меня все цветы завяли." (Because of your gasoline, all my flowers have wilted.) The man calmly replies, "Они не от бензина завяли. Цветы обычно в тех домах вянут, где атмосфера очень строгая." (They didn't wilt from gasoline. Flowers usually wilt in homes where the atmosphere is very strict.) The woman retorts sarcastically, "А у нас она слишком весёлая," (But ours is too cheerful,) listing their activities, "Вы с дядей Фёдором и в пинг-понг, и в шахматы играете, и Запорожец старый купили, развалины. Уж и не знаю, чего вам ещё не хватает." (You and Uncle Fyodor play ping-pong and chess, and you bought an old Zaporozhets, a ruin. I don't even know what else you're missing.) The boy approaches her with a blue cup, from which she drinks, her demeanor softening. The man then conspiratorially states, "Мы с дядей Фёдором посоветовались и решили... что нам совершенно необходимо где-то второго ребёнка достать. Чтобы строгость снять и сердитость." (Uncle Fyodor and I consulted and decided... that it is absolutely necessary for us to get a second child somewhere. To remove strictness and anger.) The woman responds with a startled "Ой!" (Oh!) as the boy stands content beside her, capturing a lighthearted, domestic moment amidst their cozy, colorful living room.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a cozy living room with red-striped walls, where the red-haired woman, wearing a blue vest over a white blouse and green sunglasses, sits in a yellow armchair, sipping from a blue cup. The calendar on the wall reads '31 ДЕКАБРЯ' (December 31), indicating New Year’s Eve. She emphatically states "Никогда!" (Never!) before expressing a desire to go to Prostokvashino for New Year's, but quickly adds, "А я не могу. У меня выступление на новогоднем голубом огоньке" (But I can't. I have a performance on the New Year's Blue Light). The red-haired boy enters from the kitchen, holding the same blue cup, and approaches her with a concerned expression, suggesting, "А ты садись в последнюю электричку и приезжай к нам" (Then you take the last electric train and come to us). The woman, gesturing animatedly, humorously replies that while she loves nature, it's "не до такой степени, чтобы в концертном платье в электричках разъезжать" (not to such an extent that I'd travel around in electric trains in a concert dress). Suddenly, the red-haired man enters from the right, wearing a green coat with black cuffs and a long beard, smiling warmly, and agrees, "Это верно" (That's true). He places a hand on the woman’s shoulder, and she turns to him, her expression softening. He adds that "В Простоквашино сейчас мороз" (It's freezing in Prostokvashino now) and humorously suggests wearing "концертную телогрейку... и концертные валенки" (a concert quilted jacket... and concert felt boots). The woman then expresses concern, "Ой, интересно, как там мои Шарик с Матроскиным" (Oh, I wonder how my Sharik and Matroskin are doing there). The man exits the frame, reappears holding a wooden box with Cyrillic text, and places it on a table near a bookshelf. As he opens the box, a distinct sound of handling and opening is heard, and he reads the label aloud: "Министерство связи, Простоквашинское отделение" (Ministry of Communications, Prostokvashino Branch). The woman and boy watch him with curiosity, suggesting it contains an important delivery or gift for the New Year’s Eve celebration.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a warmly lit living room with red-striped walls, where a red-haired man with a beard, wearing a green coat with black cuffs, stands beside a wooden box labeled 'МИНИСТЕРСТВО СВЯЗИ ПРОСТОКВАШИНСКОЕ ОТДЕЛЕНИЕ' (Ministry of Communications, Prostokvashino Branch). He places the box on a table covered with a green patterned cloth, next to a green rotary phone, remarking in Russian, "A talking letter. First time in my life I've received one. Wonder what's inside." A small black bird with a yellow beak perches on the box, chirping and squawking, and retrieves a white piece of paper, which the man then takes and reads with a smile as a high-pitched, distorted voice from the letter declares, "It's me, Postman Pechkin, I brought a furshnay, furfivka." The man continues to read aloud, "Sharik and Matroskin are starting to divide property," with the bird's voice interjecting quirky sounds like "Tyacheka!" and "Zapytat!" as he adds, "Soon they'll start sawing the stove... And then the hut." He concludes, "Postman Pechkin," followed by another "Tyacheka!" from the bird, before a springy sound and a clunk. The scene transitions to a snowy winter landscape outside a rustic wooden house, with snow-covered trees and gentle snowfall, accompanied by the distinct sounds of a rotary phone dialing and a fax machine whirring and clicking. The door opens to reveal a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, carrying a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He enters a dimly lit wooden room with a patchwork curtain and a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, where a gray striped cat, wearing a red hat with a white pom-pom and a red scarf, sits at the table, diligently writing with a yellow pencil on a large sheet of paper. The postal worker, gesturing with surprise, greets someone in Russian, "Oh, how good that you came, I need to send an urgent telegram to Sharik. Please, fill out the form. Felt-tip pen. Uncle Fyodor will arrive soon. Full stop. Urgently take my valenki and..." as the cat looks up with a mischievous expression, holding the pencil aloft.

segments.integrated_caption: In a dimly lit wooden room with rustic walls and a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, enters carrying a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He speaks in a deep, slightly gruff voice, mentioning '14 words and delivery' and '50 kopecks.' He holds up a yellow-framed object, then shows a piece of paper to a brown dog in a white robe and cowboy hat, perched on a stone fireplace. The dog reacts with a surprised 'Mmm?' before jumping down and, to the sound of pencil scratching, begins drawing a teepee and a Native American figure on the wall. The man watches, then turns to a gray striped cat wearing a red hat and scarf, seated at the table writing with a yellow pencil. The cat looks up, startled, then gestures animatedly, appearing to argue or explain something in a higher-pitched, somewhat whiny voice, saying, "I won't! I have no money!" The man retorts, "Look in your pockets!" The cat insists, "But I have no pockets!" The dog, now standing proudly beside his drawing, declares, "I'll draw him an answer!" The man, holding the yellow-framed object again, looks thoughtful, then raises it to his face as if taking a picture, while the cat continues to protest, "So what is this? What kind of folk art is this?" The dog proudly explains, "This is an Indian national folk hut! It's called a wigwam!" The cat, with a sigh, laments, "We, one might say, found him in a dump, washed him, cleaned him thoroughly, and he draws us a wigwam!" The cat then adds, "Ugh, Uncle Fyodor would have called a turtle instead of you for the box!" The man, holding a white box with black squares, resembling a camera or projector, gestures toward the alarmed, frantically waving cat, while the dog, still in his robe, looks on with a mix of pride and concern. The scene ends with the man in the center, holding the white box, as the dog and cat look on from either side, the room’s atmosphere tense and curious, accompanied by a whimsical, slightly melancholic accordion and string background music throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a dimly lit, rustic wooden room with dark walls, where a man with a mustache, wearing a brown hat and a long yellow coat, enters carrying a black bag labeled 'ПОЧТА' (POST). He immediately launches into a deep, somewhat preachy monologue, warning that if everyone cuts down Christmas trees for New Year, only stumps will remain, which he sarcastically notes is "good for old ladies." He is accompanied by a gray striped cat in a red hat and scarf, who sits at a wooden table with a red-and-white checkered tablecloth, appearing annoyed and crossing its arms. The cat, in a higher-pitched, whiny voice, interjects, "Why is that?" The man, gesturing animatedly, scolds the "blockhead" cat, asking what birds and hares will do without trees, and if the cat thought about them. The cat sarcastically retorts, "He thinks about hares. And who will think about us? Admiral Ivan Fyodorovich Kruzenshtern?" then genuinely inquires about Kruzenshtern's identity. The man admits he doesn't know, explaining it was just the name of a steamboat his grandmother sailed on, then emphatically praises Kruzenshtern as a "good person" who wouldn't cut down trees, "unlike some!" A brown dog in a white robe and cowboy hat perches on a stone fireplace, observing the interaction with wide eyes, occasionally pointing toward the wooden door with a concerned expression. As the man speaks, the room's lighting shifts to a warmer, golden hue. The man then holds up a yellow-framed object, possibly a camera, and later a white box with black squares, adding an element of mystery. The cat reacts with surprise and frustration, waving its paws frantically. The scene ends with the man holding the white box, while the cat continues its agitation, and the dog points again toward the door, as a clock ticks and chimes, and the man notes, "Here comes New Year soon. The clock is already striking."
